children.name: Fringilla
children.description: The chaffinches — small, brightly-patterned finches and among the commonest birds in Europe. The Common Chaffinch is one of the most abundant wild birds on Earth and a defining presence in virtually every woodland edge, park, and garden on the continent. Their song — a confident, accelerating cascade ending in a curl — is one of the earliest and most persistent sounds of the European spring. Bramblings make one of the most spectacular avian migrations, with tens of millions funnelling through Scandinavia each autumn.

children.name: Fringilla coelebs
children.description: The Eurasian chaffinch, common chaffinch, or simply the chaffinch is a common and widespread small passerine Bird – in the finch family. The male is brightly coloured with a blue-grey cap and rust-red underparts. The female is more subdued in colouring, but both sexes have two contrasting white wing bars and white sides to the tail.

children.name: Fringilla montifringilla
children.description: A boreal finch that winters in vast, irruptive flocks across Europe, tracking beech mast crops. Winter flocks in central Europe can number millions — the largest known roost held an estimated 72 million birds.

children.name: Fringilla teydea
children.description: Endemic to the Canary Islands — mainly the laurisilva forests of Tenerife and Gran Canaria. The male is a striking slate-blue; far rarer and more secretive than its mainland relative.

children.name: Fringilla polatzeki
children.description: A recently split species endemic to Gran Canaria; critically endangered with fewer than 300 individuals.

children.name: Fringilla alpicolla
children.description: A genetically distinct chaffinch population from the high Alps; proposed as a separate species.

children.name: Fringilla montalbetti
children.description: A recently split species from Corsica and Sardinia; genetically distinct.

children.name: Carduelis
children.description: The European Goldfinch and its relatives — one of the most jewel-like birds of the farmland and garden. The red-white-and-black face and vivid yellow wing-stripe make the Goldfinch instantly recognisable. It specialises in extracting seeds from thistles and teasel heads with its pointed bill — a niche no other small European bird occupies. Goldfinches were once trapped in enormous numbers for the cage-bird trade; legal protection in the 20th century allowed populations to recover substantially.

children.name: Carduelis carduelis
children.description: The European goldfinch, or simply referred to as the goldfinch, is a small passerine bird in the finch family that is native to the Palearctic zone in Europe, northern Africa, and western Asia. It has been introduced to other areas, including Australia, New Zealand, Uruguay and the United States.

children.name: Carduelis citrinella
children.description: A greenish-yellow finch of subalpine conifer forests in the mountains of central and southwestern Europe.

children.name: Carduelis carduelis caniceps
children.description: The eastern subspecies of the goldfinch; grey head replaces the European red-and-white pattern.

children.name: Carduelis lawrencei
children.description: A grey-and-yellow goldfinch of California and Baja California; named after Lawrence.

children.name: Carduelis psaltria
children.description: A small yellow-and-black finch of the western USA and Mexico; greenish back.

children.name: Carduelis notata
children.description: A small siskin from the Andes; black head and bright yellow body.

children.name: Carduelis dominicensis
children.description: A Caribbean goldfinch restricted to the island of Hispaniola.

children.name: Carduelis corsicana
children.description: A finch restricted to Corsica and Sardinia. Its grey head and yellow breast recall a muted serin.

children.name: Carduelis pinus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Carduelis tristis
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Carduelis xanthogastra
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Carduelis atrata
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Carduelis lawrencii
children.description: A grey-gold finch of California oak savannas with a tinkling song.

children.name: Spinus
children.description: Siskins — small, green-yellow finches of conifers and birches. The Eurasian Siskin is a nomadic bird, wandering across Europe in search of cone and alder crops with restless, twittering flocks. It readily visits garden feeders in winter, particularly for nyjer (thistle) seed. New World siskins include the impressive Pine Siskin and the Lawrence's Goldfinch, illustrating the wide radiation of this lineage across both hemispheres.

children.name: Spinus spinus
children.description: The Eurasian siskin is a small passerine bird in the finch family Fringillidae. It is also called the European siskin, common siskin or just siskin. Other (archaic) names include black-headed goldfinch, barley bird and aberdevine.

children.name: Spinus pinus
children.description: Spinus pinus (Pine Siskin) is a siskins species found in North America region.

children.name: Spinus tristis
children.description: A small, brightly yellow finch of North American fields and gardens, the male turning lemon-yellow with a black cap in summer; a strict seed specialist that breeds late to coincide with thistle seed.

children.name: Spinus psaltria
children.description: The lesser goldfinch is a small finch in the genus Spinus native to the Americas.

children.name: Spinus magellanicus
children.description: The most widespread siskin in South America, occurring from Colombia to Tierra del Fuego in open woodland, scrub, and gardens. Males wear a black hood contrasting with bright yellow underparts. Highly gregarious, it often forms large feeding flocks in weedy fields and shrubby hillsides.

children.name: Spinus cucullatus
children.description: A brilliant scarlet-and-black finch native to northern Venezuela, Colombia, and Trinidad, now critically endangered due to intensive trapping for the cage-bird trade. Its red pigment genes were historically introgressed into domestic canary bloodlines to produce the orange-red canary. Wild populations have been reduced by 80% since the 1940s.

children.name: Spinus lawrencei
children.description: A small grey finch with vivid yellow wing patches and a bright yellow breast band, breeding in the dry foothills of California and Baja California. Highly nomadic outside the breeding season, it concentrates at water sources in the Arizona desert and northern Mexico. Named after the prominent American ornithologist George Newbold Lawrence.

children.name: Spinus atratus
children.description: A largely black siskin with bold yellow wing patches, found at high altitudes in the Andes from Peru to northwestern Argentina. Males are striking all-black birds that flash yellow in flight, foraging in mixed flocks on Andean grasslands and rocky slopes above 3,500 m.

children.name: Spinus uropygialis
children.description: A dark-backed Andean siskin found from Ecuador and Peru to northern Argentina, inhabiting open scrub, grassland, and cultivation at high elevations. The vivid yellow rump is conspicuous in flight, readily separating it from other black-and-yellow siskins.

children.name: Spinus notatus
children.description: A Central American siskin with a distinctive black hood and vivid yellow underparts, inhabiting montane pine and mixed forests from Mexico to Honduras. Often encountered in small flocks feeding on pine seeds and weed seeds along forest edges and roadsides.

children.name: Spinus xanthogastrus
children.description: A small siskin with a black back and vivid yellow underparts, inhabiting humid montane forests from Costa Rica south to Bolivia. It frequents forest edges and clearings in the subtropical zone and is often encountered in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Spinus spinescens
children.description: A high-altitude siskin of the northern Andes in Colombia and western Venezuela, inhabiting paramo grasslands and adjacent scrub near the treeline. Males are olive-green above and yellow below, blending into the yellowed bunch-grasses of their high-elevation home.

children.name: Spinus barbatus
children.description: A siskin from the southern Andes; black chin contrasts with yellow body.

children.name: Spinus crassirostris
children.description: Found in the high Andes; unusually heavy bill for seed-cracking in harsh puna habitats.

children.name: Spinus olivaceus
children.description: A dull olive-green siskin from the eastern Andes of Peru and Bolivia.

children.name: Spinus xanthophthalmus
children.description: A striking black-and-yellow siskin from the Peruvian Andes.

children.name: Spinus atriceps
children.description: A small siskin from the mountains of Venezuela; black cap and green body.

children.name: Spinus lawrencii
children.description: A small grey-gold finch of California's oak savannas. Its tinkling, canary-like song and nomadic wandering make it a special find for birders.

children.name: Spinus notata
children.description: A siskin of Central American highlands with a black cap and bright yellow body.

children.name: Spinus barbata
children.description: A high-Andean siskin with a black chin and bright yellow underparts.

children.name: Spinus atrata
children.description: A unique, almost entirely black siskin of the high Peruvian Andes.

children.name: Spinus dominicensis
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Spinus siemiradzkii
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Spinus spinecens
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Spinus yarrellii
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Chloris
children.description: Greenfinches — stout, heavy-billed finches that favour gardens, farmland hedges, and scrub. The European Greenfinch was a common garden bird across much of Europe until a trichomonosis epidemic from 2005 onwards caused population crashes of up to 65% in some countries. Despite this, it remains widespread. Its distinctive descending 'dzeee' call and wheezing song are familiar sounds of suburban gardens. The bill is large and powerful enough to crack large seeds and even olive stones.

children.name: Chloris chloris
children.description: The European greenfinch or simply the greenfinch is a small passerine bird in the finch family Fringillidae.

children.name: Chloris sinica
children.description: A greenfinch of East Asia with bright yellow wing flashes, common in woodland edges, parks, and farmland from Japan to China.

children.name: Chloris spinoides
children.description: A Himalayan greenfinch of oak and rhododendron forest from Pakistan to Vietnam, readily distinguished from the European Greenfinch by its vivid yellow underparts and broad yellow wing-patches. It inhabits forest edges and cultivation from 1,500 to 3,500 m elevation.

children.name: Chloris ambigua
children.description: A dark-headed greenfinch of the eastern Himalayas and southern China, found in open forest and scrub at moderate to high elevations. The male's black hood gives it a more dramatic appearance than other greenfinches; it is often encountered in mixed-species flocks in winter.

children.name: Chloris monguilloti
children.description: A rare and range-restricted greenfinch endemic to the Da Lat Plateau of southern Vietnam, found in highland pine forest above 1,500 m. First described in 1926 and one of the most poorly known greenfinches, its population status and ecology remain largely unstudied.

children.name: Chloris kittlitzi
children.description: An endangered greenfinch from the Ogasawara Islands of Japan; recently recognised as distinct.

children.name: Chloris ambianus
children.description: A greenfinch from the Philippines; distinctive black chin on the male.

children.name: Chloris monguilii
children.description: A greenfinch of the Himalayan foothills, from Nepal to Myanmar. Its plumage is more yellow than the European species.

children.name: Pyrrhula
children.description: Bullfinches — compact, rounded finches with a stubby, rounded bill and strikingly patterned plumage. The Eurasian Bullfinch male is one of the most vividly coloured small birds in Europe — a claret-red breast, jet black cap, and white rump visible as it flies away. Bullfinches are quiet and often unobtrusive; their plaintive, piping call is the most reliable signal of their presence. They have a specialised tongue and palate for processing seeds by husking them, rather than cracking them.

children.name: Pyrrhula pyrrhula
children.description: Bullfinches were once persecuted in orchards as crop pests — they eat buds in winter when other food is scarce. Populations declined markedly in the late 20th century as farmland hedgerows were removed.

children.name: Pyrrhula nipalensis
children.description: A subtly coloured bullfinch of Himalayan and Southeast Asian montane forests, browner than its European relatives.

children.name: Pyrrhula aurantiaca
children.description: A vivid orange-breasted bullfinch endemic to the western Himalayas from Pakistan to northwest India, found in conifer forests and mixed scrub between 2,000 and 3,500 m. One of the most brilliantly coloured of all bullfinches and a sought-after bird among visiting observers.

children.name: Pyrrhula erythrocephala
children.description: A strikingly coloured bullfinch of dense Himalayan forests from Nepal to southwest China, the male bearing a brick-red head that contrasts sharply with grey upperparts and a pale rump. Inhabits rhododendron and mixed broadleaf forests between 2,500 and 4,000 m.

children.name: Pyrrhula erythaca
children.description: A grey-headed bullfinch of montane conifer and bamboo forests in the eastern Himalayas and southern China. It inhabits dense undergrowth along forest streams and is often encountered in small parties foraging quietly at mid-storey level.

children.name: Pyrrhula leucogenys
children.description: A Philippine bullfinch; white cheeks and grey-pink body; inhabits montane forests.

children.name: Pyrrhula griseiventris
children.description: Found from the Himalayas to Southeast Asia; pale grey belly distinguishes it from P. pyrrhula.

children.name: Pyrrhula leucogenis
children.description: A bullfinch of the Philippines with white cheeks contrasting with the black head.

children.name: Pyrrhula aurantia
children.description: A beautiful orange-breasted bullfinch of the Himalayan region.

children.name: Pyrrhula erythrops
children.description: A small bullfinch of Southeast Asian mountains with white-spotted wing coverts.

children.name: Pyrrhula nigra
children.description: A largely black bullfinch of the Himalayas, with only a hint of rosy colour on the breast.

children.name: Pyrrhula murina
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Loxia
children.description: Crossbills — among the most specialised of all finches, with uniquely crossed bill tips evolved to prize open conifer cones. The crossing of the tips can go either right-over-left or left-over-right; the ratio varies by population. Crossbills are nomadic to an extreme degree, following cone crops across boreal and montane forests. In irruption years they appear far outside their normal range. They are among the very few birds that can breed in any month including mid-winter, timed to peak cone availability.

children.name: Loxia curvirostra
children.description: Specialised on Norway spruce cones. Bill morphology varies between populations matched to local cone species — a case study in rapid ecological speciation. The sound of their feeding — a rain of cone scales — often reveals a flock before it is seen.

children.name: Loxia pytyopsittacus
children.description: The largest crossbill, with a massive parrot-like bill adapted for the hard cones of Scots pine. Specialist of northern boreal forests; its rarity in Britain depends on irruption events from Scandinavia.

children.name: Loxia leucoptera
children.description: Recognised by two bold white wing-bars. Specialist of larch forests; irruptions into western Europe occur when larch cone crops fail in Siberia. The male has a pinkish-red rather than brick-red plumage.

children.name: Loxia scotica
children.description: The UK's only endemic bird species, a crossbill of Caledonian pine forests in the Scottish Highlands, distinguished by its intermediate bill size and diagnostic flight calls.

children.name: Loxia patagonica
children.description: A South American crossbill from the Andes of Chile and Argentina; feeds on Araucaria seeds.

children.name: Loxia ancora
children.description: Endemic to Hispaniola; the only Caribbean crossbill; critically endangered by deforestation.

children.name: Loxia megaplaga
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Loxia sinesciurus
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Coccothraustes
children.description: The Hawfinch — the largest finch in Europe, with a massive, pointed, steel-grey bill capable of cracking cherry and olive stones with forces up to 50 kg. Despite its size and striking plumage — orange-brown with a black mask, blue-black wings, and a short white-tipped tail — the Hawfinch is notoriously shy and difficult to observe, keeping to high canopy in mature broadleaf woodland. In winter, small parties gather at hornbeam and wild cherry trees to crack the seeds.

children.name: Coccothraustes coccothraustes
children.description: Generates a bite force equivalent to lifting a 5 kg weight — used to crack cherry stones for the kernels inside. The bill transitions from a pale greyish-blue in breeding season to a pale yellow-horn in winter.

children.name: Linaria
children.description: Linnets and redpolls — small, streaky brown finches of open country, heathland, and scrub. The Common Linnet's song is one of the most cheerful sounds of a sunny heath or clifftop in spring. The Twite is a montane equivalent, breeding on moorland and coastal salt-marshes. This group has declined markedly across western Europe due to changes in farming practice — specifically the loss of seed-bearing weeds from cultivated fields.

children.name: Linaria cannabina
children.description: The breeding male has a crimson forehead and rosy-pink breast — plumage acquired in spring through feather wear rather than a moult. A Red List species in the UK due to severe population decline since the 1970s.

children.name: Linaria flavirostris
children.description: Linaria flavirostris (Twite) is a linnets species found in Europe and Asia regions.

children.name: Linaria yemenensis
children.description: A small finch of the Yemeni highlands, restricted to the mountains of the Arabian Peninsula.

children.name: Acanthis
children.description: Redpolls — small Arctic and boreal finches with a crimson forehead spot and pink-flushed breast in breeding males. Redpoll taxonomy is disputed: some authorities treat Common, Lesser, and Arctic Redpolls as one species with extreme plumage variation, while others recognise three. They are irruptive nomads, appearing in lowland Europe in large numbers in some winters (when birch cone crops fail in Scandinavia) and almost absent in others.

children.name: Acanthis flammea
children.description: A classic irruptive finch — abundant in some winters, virtually absent in others. Flocks move and feed with great restlessness, hanging acrobatically from birch and alder catkins.

children.name: Acanthis hornemanni
children.description: A pale, frosty redpoll of the high Arctic tundra and taiga, paler and fluffier than the Common Redpoll.

children.name: Acanthis cabaret
children.description: A small, compact and distinctly dark redpoll of birch scrub, alder carr, and garden feeding stations in northwestern Europe, particularly Britain, Ireland, and the Alps. Long treated as a subspecies of Common Redpoll, it is now widely recognised as a full species on the basis of consistent morphological and vocal differences.

children.name: Serinus
children.description: Small streaky yellow finches of open and scrubby habitats across Europe and Africa.

children.name: Serinus serinus
children.description: The smallest European finch, a streaky yellow bird with a jingling song, expanding northward through gardens, orchards, and parks.

children.name: Serinus canaria
children.description: The wild ancestor of the domestic canary, native to the Canary Islands, Azores, and Madeira, found in open woodland and cultivation.

children.name: Serinus pusillus
children.description: A small, high-altitude serin of subalpine scrub and rocky slopes from Turkey and the Caucasus east to Afghanistan and Pakistan, with a distinctive orange-red forehead and heavily streaked plumage. It breeds above the treeline and descends to valleys and foothills in winter.

children.name: Serinus syriacus
children.description: A pale, yellowish serin restricted to mountainous scrub in Lebanon, Syria, Israel, and Jordan, wintering in northeast Africa. Its small and fragmented breeding range makes it one of the more vulnerable serins, threatened by habitat loss in the eastern Mediterranean.

children.name: Serinus alario
children.description: A striking black-and-white canary from southern Africa; the male has a black head and throat.

children.name: Serinus leucopygius
children.description: A small seedeater from the Sahel; white rump visible in flight.

children.name: Serinus reichardi
children.description: A seedeater from the highlands of Ethiopia and Eritrea.

children.name: Serinus flavicola
children.description: A bright yellow-bellied species from East African savannas.

children.name: Serinus thibetanus
children.description: A small, streaked serin of high-altitude scrub on the Tibetan Plateau and adjacent Himalayas, breeding up to 4,500 m. Its ecology remains poorly studied.

children.name: Serinus estherae
children.description: A serin of montane forest and scrub in the mountains of Southeast Asia, found in the Philippines, Borneo, Sulawesi, and Sumatra above 1,000 m.

children.name: Crithagra
children.description: African seedeaters and canaries of savanna, scrub, and woodland.

children.name: Crithagra mozambica
children.description: A common African canary with a bright yellow face and grey crown, widespread in savanna and gardens across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Crithagra flaviventris
children.description: A widespread and distinctive canary of southern Africa, the male brilliant yellow with olive-grey upperparts and subtle streaking. Common in dry scrub, gardens, farmland, and fynbos across the western half of the region; one of the most familiar songbirds in the southern African lowlands.

children.name: Crithagra sulphurata
children.description: A large, robust canary of forest edges, dense scrub, and riparian thickets from east Africa south to the Cape, distinguished by its heavy bill and yellowish-green plumage. Its loud, complex, and sustained song is a striking feature of montane thickets and forest margins.

children.name: Crithagra leucopterus
children.description: A small, streaky seedeater of rocky slopes, open scrub, and highland grassland in equatorial Africa, notable for its bold white wing patches that flash conspicuously in flight. Often encountered in small flocks feeding on grass seeds and weed seeds in disturbed areas.

children.name: Crithagra striolatus
children.description: A heavily streaked and extremely common seedeater of highland scrub, gardens, and forest edges from Ethiopia to South Africa. One of the most abundant and vocal seedeaters in East African highland towns and roadsides, its song a persistent jangle from roadside shrubs.

children.name: Crithagra citrinipectus
children.description: A small canary with a lemon-yellow breast restricted to lowland coastal scrub and miombo woodland edges in Mozambique and the adjacent Limpopo province of South Africa. Despite its unassuming appearance it is a range-restricted species of conservation interest.

children.name: Crithagra alario
children.description: A boldly patterned canary of the Karoo and Namib scrublands of southern Africa, the male displaying a clean black hood contrasting with a chestnut back and white underparts. Endemic to the arid west of southern Africa, it feeds on seeds of annual plants and is strongly irruptive in response to rainfall.

children.name: Crithagra albogularis
children.description: A large, pale canary of southern African scrubland, coastal fynbos, and Karoo, distinguished by its white throat and faint greenish wash on the upperparts. Often one of the most conspicuous birds in arid western South African habitats, perching prominently on low shrubs while singing.

children.name: Crithagra scotops
children.description: A secretive, olive-green canary endemic to the evergreen forests and dense scrub of southern Africa from the Cape Peninsula to Zimbabwe. Despite its retiring habits in dense undergrowth, its loud and complex song carries well through the forest, often being the first indication of its presence.

children.name: Crithagra atrogularis
children.description: A widespread southern African canary with a black throat patch.

children.name: Crithagra reichelarum
children.description: A West African canary; named after the ornithologist Anton Reichenow.

children.name: Crithagra gularis
children.description: A streaky brown canary of southern African woodlands and gardens. Its powerful conical bill can crack the hardest seeds.

children.name: Crithagra reichenowi
children.description: A canary of the East African coastal forests, named after the German ornithologist Anton Reichenow.

children.name: Crithagra mennelli
children.description: A little-known canary of the miombo woodlands of central and eastern Africa.

children.name: Crithagra strigosus
children.description: A heavily streaked brown canary of South African fynbos and karoo shrublands.

children.name: Crithagra citrinelloides
children.description: A bright yellow-green seedeater of East African montane forests and their edges, from Ethiopia south to Zimbabwe. The male's vivid yellow-green plumage and black mask make it one of the most colourful canaries in Africa.

children.name: Crithagra hyposticta
children.description: A small, lightly streaked canary restricted to papyrus swamps and adjacent lake shores in Uganda, Rwanda, and the Democratic Republic of Congo.

children.name: Crithagra dorsostriata
children.description: A common canary of dry acacia scrub and savanna across East Africa, recognised by its clean white belly contrasting with streaked grey-brown upperparts.

children.name: Crithagra burtoni
children.description: A dark, heavily built seedeater of montane forest edges across eastern and central Africa, its unusually robust bill capable of crushing large seeds.

children.name: Crithagra melanochroa
children.description: A poorly known, dark seedeater restricted to the Kipengere and Livingstone mountain ranges of southern Tanzania, above 1,800 m.

children.name: Crithagra tristriata
children.description: A lightly built seedeater of highland scrub and cultivated areas in Ethiopia and Eritrea, with three pale stripes on the head.

children.name: Crithagra xanthopygia
children.description: A seedeater of the Ethiopian highlands with a distinctive yellow rump that flashes conspicuously in flight.

children.name: Crithagra capistrata
children.description: A canary of dense miombo woodland in central Africa, the male bearing a striking black face mask against vivid yellowish-green underparts.

children.name: Crithagra whytii
children.description: A small seedeater of highland scrub and forest edges in eastern and central Africa, identified by its pale yellow supercilium.

children.name: Crithagra ankoberensis
children.description: One of the rarest canaries in the world, endemic to a tiny area of cliff-face scrub near Ankober in Ethiopia. Discovered in 1976; Endangered due to its minuscule range.

children.name: Crithagra flavigula
children.description: A localised seedeater of dry scrub and rocky hillsides in Ethiopia and Eritrea, its yellow throat patch the most distinctive field mark.

children.name: Crithagra menachensis
children.description: A small serin endemic to the highlands of Yemen and adjacent southwestern Saudi Arabia, inhabiting terraced cultivation and highland scrub.

children.name: Crithagra xantholaema
children.description: A small, yellow-throated seedeater of the Sahel and dry Sudan savanna zone from Senegal to Ethiopia, favouring dry acacia scrub.

children.name: Crithagra rufobrunnea
children.description: A small seedeater endemic to the island of São Tomé in the Gulf of Guinea, inhabiting forest undergrowth, secondary growth, and gardens.

children.name: Crithagra totta
children.description: A small finch of rocky montane fynbos and scrub, endemic to the mountains of the Western Cape, South Africa. It feeds on seeds and invertebrates among proteas and ericas.

children.name: Haemorhous
children.description: New World rosefinches with red-washed males, common in North American habitats.

children.name: Haemorhous mexicanus
children.description: A widespread North American finch with a red-flushed head and breast in males; introduced to the eastern US and now abundant in urban areas.

children.name: Haemorhous purpureus
children.description: The purple finch is a bird in the finch family, Fringillidae. It breeds in the northern United States, southern Canada, and the west coast of North America.

children.name: Haemorhous cassinii
children.description: A montane finch of Rocky Mountain conifer forests, the male bearing a deeper crimson crown contrasting with a paler pink breast than the similar Purple Finch. Named after the prominent American ornithologist John Cassin; it specialises in high-elevation pine and fir forests from British Columbia to New Mexico.

children.name: Haemorhous aneus
children.description: A colourful finch of western Mexican forests; the male is bright yellow with black wings.

children.name: Carpodacus
children.description: Old World rosefinches of mountains and northern forests, the males richly red.

children.name: Carpodacus erythrinus
children.description: A migratory finch whose breeding males are washed crimson on head and breast; expanding westward across Europe from its Asian core.

children.name: Carpodacus rubicilla
children.description: The largest rosefinch, a deep-pink high-altitude bird of rocky alpine zones in the Caucasus and Central Asian mountains.

children.name: Carpodacus pulcherrimus
children.description: One of the commonest rosefinches of the Himalayas and Central Asian mountains, the males displaying a delicate carmine-red head and breast contrasting with streaked brown upperparts. Breeds in high-altitude scrub and subalpine meadows, descending in flocks to lower elevations and valley floors in winter.

children.name: Carpodacus rhodochrous
children.description: A subtly beautiful rosefinch of Himalayan rhododendron scrub and conifer forests from Pakistan to Nepal, the male deep pink with a prominent pale supercilium. Inhabits dense montane forest between 2,500 and 4,000 m, descending to lower altitudes in severe winters.

children.name: Carpodacus roseus
children.description: A pale pink rosefinch breeding in Siberian taiga and birch woodland that irrupts westward into Europe in certain winters when food supplies in the east fail. First described by the German naturalist Peter Simon Pallas from specimens collected in Russia; the male is washed in soft rose-pink.

children.name: Carpodacus synoicus
children.description: A desert-adapted rosefinch of rocky wadis, boulder-strewn slopes, and arid mountains in the Sinai Peninsula, Arabia, and northwest India. Males are washed pale pink providing excellent camouflage against sandstone; females are sandy-buff, blending seamlessly into their rocky desert environment.

children.name: Carpodacus puniceus
children.description: The largest Asian rosefinch, a hefty bird of high alpine terrain in the Himalayas and Tibetan Plateau above 4,000 m. Males bear a brilliant crimson-red forehead that contrasts with grey-brown upperparts; it is among the highest-altitude breeding finches in the world.

children.name: Carpodacus nipalensis
children.description: A dark, richly coloured rosefinch of Himalayan rhododendron and mixed broadleaf forests, the male deep crimson with blackish-brown upperparts. A resident species at mid to high elevations from Nepal through northeast India to southwest China.

children.name: Carpodacus thura
children.description: A high-altitude rosefinch of scrubby alpine zones from Afghanistan and Pakistan east to southwest China, the male identified by a bold white supercilium and pinkish-white underparts. A common and conspicuous bird in Himalayan scrub between 3,500 and 5,000 m, moving to lower elevations in winter.

children.name: Carpodacus edwardsii
children.description: A poorly known rosefinch of dense rhododendron and juniper scrub in the eastern Himalayas and southwest China. The male is deep wine-red throughout; its ecology and population status remain relatively understudied compared to other Himalayan rosefinches.

children.name: Carpodacus waltoni
children.description: A Tibetan rosefinch; named after the British ornithologist.

children.name: Carpodacus verreauxii
children.description: A Himalayan rosefinch from the western part of the range; rich crimson male plumage.

children.name: Carpodacus rubicinoides
children.description: A rosefinch of the Tibetan plateau with heavy streaking on the underparts.

children.name: Carpodacus trifasciatus
children.description: A rosefinch of the Chinese mountains with three white wing bars.

children.name: Carpodacus rodopeplus
children.description: A rosefinch of the eastern Himalayas with white-spotted wing coverts.

children.name: Carpodacus vernayi
children.description: A rosefinch of the northern Myanmar mountains, named after the naturalist Arthur Vernay.

children.name: Carpodacus stoliczkae
children.description: A pale, washed-out rosefinch of rocky Central Asian steppe and semi-desert from Iran east through Kazakhstan to western China. Males are delicately washed pink on the head and rump.

children.name: Carpodacus vinaceus
children.description: A rosefinch of montane forests in Taiwan and southern China, the male a deep wine-red with subtle pale streaking. Common in the central mountains of Taiwan between 2,000 and 3,500 m.

children.name: Carpodacus formosanus
children.description: A rosefinch endemic to the high mountains of Taiwan. The male is deep crimson with a pale streaked breast; inhabits subalpine conifer forest between 2,500 and 3,900 m.

children.name: Carpodacus davidianus
children.description: A rosefinch of the mountains of central and northern China, closely related to the Beautiful Rosefinch. Named after the French missionary-naturalist Père Armand David.

children.name: Carpodacus severtzovi
children.description: A rosefinch of rocky slopes and scrub in the Tian Shan and western Himalayas, named after the Russian naturalist Nikolai Severtzov.

children.name: Pinicola
children.description: A single large, plump boreal finch of northern conifer forests worldwide.

children.name: Pinicola enucleator
children.description: A large, sluggish rosy-red finch of the boreal taiga across the Northern Hemisphere, feeding on conifer seeds, buds, and berries.

children.name: Leucosticte
children.description: Rosy finches — hardy montane finches of high-altitude snowfields, glaciers, and rocky alpine tundra in Asia and North America. They are among the few birds that spend the entire year above the treeline, foraging at the snow's edge for windblown seeds and invertebrates. Males develop a variable pink or rosy wash on the wings and rump.

children.name: Leucosticte tephrocotis
children.description: A high-mountain finch of Alaska and the western mountains of North America, breeding in crevices near permanent snowfields and glaciers. The grey crown patch distinguishes it from the other North American rosy finches; it forages on windblown seeds and invertebrates exposed at the retreating snow edge.

children.name: Leucosticte atrata
children.description: The darkest of the North American rosy finches, breeding on high rocky ridges of the central Rocky Mountains above timberline. Largely black with contrasting pink wing patches, it winters in mixed-species rosy finch flocks in lower-elevation basins and desert scrub.

children.name: Leucosticte australis
children.description: The most restricted of the North American rosy finches, endemic to the southern Rocky Mountains of Colorado and New Mexico. Brown-capped with a rich pink flush on the wings, it breeds at higher elevations than almost any other North American songbird and is vulnerable to climate change affecting its alpine habitat.

children.name: Leucosticte arctoa
children.description: A rosy finch of Central Asian mountain ranges from the Altai and Tian Shan to northeast China, wintering in flocks on steppe, farmland, and lower mountain slopes. Closely related to the North American rosy finches and equally hardy in extreme cold, its seven subspecies span a wide range of plumage variation.

children.name: Leucosticte brandti
children.description: A high-altitude finch of Central Asian mountains; grey-brown with pink wing patches.

children.name: Leucosticte nemoricola
children.description: Widespread in the Himalayas; streaked brown finch of alpine meadows above treeline.

children.name: Leucosticte sillemi
children.description: One of the world's least-known birds, collected by J.A. Sillem in 1929 in western Tibet and not confirmed again until 2012. Described as a species only in 1992 from the original specimens.

children.name: Mycerobas
children.description: Allied grosbeaks — large, heavy-billed finches of Himalayan and Central Asian montane forests. The males are boldly patterned in black and yellow or red, and the massive bill is adapted for crushing large seeds and hard fruit stones. The genus is restricted to the montane forest zone of Asia from the western Himalayas east to China.

children.name: Mycerobas carnipes
children.description: A large, chunky grosbeak of Himalayan and Central Asian conifer forests, the male bearing bold white wing patches that flash in flight against his black and yellow plumage. The most widespread Mycerobas grosbeak, ranging from Afghanistan to China; feeds on juniper and pine seeds that it cracks with its massive bill.

children.name: Mycerobas affinis
children.description: A large and brilliantly coloured grosbeak of the eastern Himalayas and southern China, the male glowing yellow with a jet-black head, back, and wings. Inhabits dense montane broadleaf and mixed forest between 2,000 and 3,500 m; feeds on large seeds and fruits including those of laurels and maples.

children.name: Mycerobas melanozanthos
children.description: A striking grosbeak of oak and conifer forests in the Himalayas and southwest China, the male black with yellow underparts and distinctive white spots across the wing. Often encountered at forest fruiting trees, where its loud calls betray its presence in the dense canopy.

children.name: Mycerobas icterioides
children.description: A large montane grosbeak of the western Himalayas from Pakistan to western Nepal, inhabiting mixed conifer and oak forests between 2,500 and 4,000 m. The male is boldly patterned black and yellow, similar to the Collared Grosbeak but replacing it at the western end of the Himalayan chain.

children.name: Eophona
children.description: Japanese and Chinese grosbeaks — large, stocky finches of East Asian broadleaf and mixed forests with massive orange-yellow bills. They are powerful seed and cherry-stone crackers, the bill providing enough force to open hard fruit kernels unavailable to smaller finches. Both species have a black head and pale grey body, making them among the most striking finches in Asia.

children.name: Eophona migratoria
children.description: A large, compact grosbeak breeding in northeast China, Korea, and adjacent Russia, migrating south in winter to eastern China and Japan. The male has a jet-black head and gleaming yellow-orange bill, foraging on large seeds and tree fruits in broadleaf and mixed forest.

children.name: Eophona personata
children.description: The largest Old World grosbeak, a stocky bird of mature Japanese and Korean broadleaf forests with a particularly massive orange-yellow bill and a black hood. A specialist seed and cherry-stone cracker, it moves between Japan, Korea, and eastern China in winter and is a regular visitor to parks and wooded gardens.

children.name: Hesperiphona
children.description: New World grosbeaks of North and Central American conifer forests, closely related to the Old World grosbeaks. The males are dramatically patterned in yellow and black with white wing patches. These are irruptive birds, appearing in large numbers well outside their core range in years of food scarcity.

children.name: Hesperiphona vespertina
children.description: A large, irruptive finch of northern and montane North American conifer forests, the male bearing vivid yellow and black plumage with a bold white wing patch. Its name derives from a historical — and erroneous — belief that it only sang at dusk. Populations have declined substantially since the 1980s, likely linked to the decline of spruce budworm irruptions that once triggered mass movements.

children.name: Hesperiphona abeillei
children.description: The hooded grosbeak or Albiellé's grosbeak is a passerine bird in the family Fringillidae, the finches and euphonias. It is found in Guatemala, El Salvador, and Mexico.

children.name: Bucanetes
children.description: Trumpeter and Mongolian finches — small, sandy-coloured desert finches of arid and semi-arid regions from the Canary Islands and North Africa across the Middle East to Central Asia. Males have a pink or rosy wash on the plumage and bill. Their distinctive calls, often described as nasal or trumpet-like, carry well across open desert terrain.

children.name: Bucanetes githagineus
children.description: A sandy-pink finch of desert, arid stony plains, and dry gullies from the Canary Islands and North Africa across the Middle East to northwest India. The male's nasal, buzzing call has been likened to a small tin trumpet. Breeds in rock crevices or under boulders and can be remarkably confiding around desert habitations.

children.name: Bucanetes mongolicus
children.description: A pale, desert-adapted finch of Central Asian rocky steppe and semi-desert from Iran and Afghanistan east to Mongolia. Closely resembles the Trumpeter Finch but is larger and stouter-billed, and the two species replace each other across a contact zone in Central Asia.

children.name: Drepanis
children.description: Hawaiian mamos and the Iiwi — iconic Hawaiian honeycreepers with long, curved bills evolved for accessing the nectar of native lobeliad flowers. The Iiwi is one of the most recognisable birds in the Hawaiian archipelago; its brilliant scarlet plumage and sickle-shaped bill make it unmistakable. Hawaiian honeycreepers are now placed within Fringillidae based on genomic data.

children.name: Drepanis coccinea
children.description: One of the most iconic Hawaiian birds, the Iiwi is a brilliant scarlet bird with a long, deeply curved salmon-coloured bill evolved for feeding on the nectar of native lobeliad flowers. Highly susceptible to avian malaria, it has been largely eliminated from lower elevations and now survives mainly in high-altitude ohia forest on Hawaii, Maui, and Kauai. Its decline is considered a sentinel of broader Hawaiian forest ecosystem collapse.

children.name: Drepanis pacifica
children.description: An extinct honeycreeper with a long curved bill; its yellow feathers were used for Hawaiian royal cloaks.

children.name: Drepanis funerea
children.description: An extinct Hawaiian honeycreeper from Molokai, last seen in 1907. Entirely black with a long, deeply curved bill for nectar extraction. Its black feathers were harvested for traditional Hawaiian royal regalia.

children.name: Himatione
children.description: The Apapane — a crimson Hawaiian honeycreeper that is among the most abundant of all surviving Hawaiian forest birds. Unlike many of its relatives, the Apapane has maintained larger populations in part due to its ability to exploit a wider range of ohia habitat.

children.name: Himatione sanguinea
children.description: The most abundant of the Hawaiian honeycreepers, the Apapane is a bright crimson bird with white undertail coverts and a slightly curved black bill, found in native ohia lehua forest across the main Hawaiian islands. Its seasonal movements track the flowering of ohia trees, and its loud, bubbling song and whirring wings are characteristic sounds of Hawaiian montane forest.

children.name: Himatione fraithii
children.description: An extinct Hawaiian honeycreeper from Laysan; vivid crimson red with white eye-ring.

children.name: Loxops
children.description: Small Hawaiian honeycreepers with a unique bill shape where the tips cross slightly.

children.name: Loxops coccineus
children.description: A small Hawaiian honeycreeper with a unique crossed bill tip for prying open leaf buds.

children.name: Loxops caeruleirostris
children.description: The akekeʻe or Kauaʻi ʻākepa is a bird species in the family Fringillidae, where it is placed in the Hawaiian honeycreeper genus Loxops. It is endemic to the island of Kauaʻi where it is found in small numbers in higher elevations. Because of their similar size, shape, and unusual bill, the akekeʻe and the Hawaiʻi ʻākepa were for some time classified as a single species.

children.name: Loxops mana
children.description: A small, dull-green Hawaiian honeycreeper of mature ohia and koa forest on the Big Island, creeping along branches like a nuthatch to find insects in bark crevices. Endangered by avian malaria.

children.name: Loxops ochraceus
children.description: A small creeper of Maui's native forests, foraging for insects and spiders on ohia and koa bark. Vulnerable to avian malaria spread by introduced mosquitoes.

children.name: Hemignathus
children.description: Hawaiian honeycreepers with extremely long curved bills for extracting nectar from lobelioid flowers.

children.name: Hemignathus wilsoni
children.description: The ʻakiapōlāʻau, also known as the Hawaiian woodpecker, is a species of Hawaiian honeycreeper that is endemic to the island of Hawaii. Its natural habitats are dry and montane moist forests, and it is the only bird species on the island to occupy the woodpecker niche. The bird is 5.5 inches (14 cm) in length, and has an unusually curved beak-(a specialist species).

children.name: Hemignathus munroi
children.description: A small generalist honeycreeper; one of the most adaptable Hawaiian finches.

children.name: Hemignathus lucidus
children.description: A critically endangered Hawaiian honeycreeper with a dramatically asymmetric bill — the upper mandible long and decurved for probing bark, the shorter lower mandible used for hammering. If any survive, only a handful remain on Kauai.

children.name: Hemignathus ellisianus
children.description: An extinct Hawaiian honeycreeper with an extremely long, sickle-shaped bill. Once found on Oahu, Lanai, and Maui; last definitively recorded in 1940.

children.name: Spiza
children.description: A grassland bird of the central USA; the sole member of its genus; winters in large flocks in South America.

children.name: Spiza americana
children.description: A common grassland bird of the central USA; brilliant yellow breast and black V.

children.name: Telespiza
children.description: Laysan and Nihoa finches of remote northwestern Hawaiian atolls, evolved as omnivorous survivors on tiny coral islands with limited resources.

children.name: Telespiza cantans
children.description: A large, robust finch endemic to Laysan Island in the Northwestern Hawaiian Islands. Feeds on seeds, insects, eggs, and carrion. Critically endangered; nearly extirpated when introduced rabbits destroyed its habitat in the early 20th century.

children.name: Telespiza ultima
children.description: A seed-eating finch restricted entirely to Nihoa Island, a 63-hectare volcanic islet in the Northwestern Hawaiian Islands. The entire world population lives on this remote sea rock.

children.name: Palmeria
children.description: The Akohekohe — one of the most spectacular Hawaiian honeycreepers, with an extraordinary crest and vivid orange-red plumage patches.

children.name: Palmeria dolei
children.description: The ʻākohekohe, or crested honeycreeper, is a species of Hawaiian honeycreeper. It is endemic to the island of Maui in Hawaiʻi. The ʻākohekohe is susceptible to mosquito‐transmitted avian malaria and only breeds in high‐elevation wet forests.

children.name: Pseudonestor
children.description: The Maui Parrotbill — a critically endangered Hawaiian honeycreeper with a massively powerful, parrot-like bill for tearing apart wood to find beetle larvae.

children.name: Pseudonestor xanthophrys
children.description: One of the most specialised and endangered birds in the world, using its extraordinary hooked bill to rip open branches and extract wood-boring beetle larvae. Fewer than 500 individuals remain, all in a few thousand hectares of native forest on Maui.

children.name: Melamprosops
children.description: The Poo-uli — discovered in 1973, extinct by 2004. It represented a unique evolutionary lineage with no close relatives among known Hawaiian birds.

children.name: Melamprosops phaeosoma
children.description: Discovered in 1973 and extinct by 2004. Fed primarily on land snails and arthropods. By 2004 only three individuals were known; the last died in captivity on 26 November 2004. Molecular analysis showed it was the most genetically distinct of all Hawaiian honeycreepers.

children.name: Oreomystis
children.description: The Akikiki — a tiny bark-creeping honeycreeper confined to Kauai's native forest, now one of the most critically endangered birds in the world.

children.name: Oreomystis bairdi
children.description: A tiny, pale grey creeper of Kauai's Alaka'i Wilderness. Critically endangered with fewer than 50 individuals as of 2020, primarily threatened by the expansion of avian malaria into its high-elevation refugia.

children.name: Paroreomyza
children.description: Hawaiian creepers — small bark-foraging honeycreepers, several species of which are now extinct due to introduced rats and avian disease.

children.name: Paroreomyza maculata
children.description: An extinct Hawaiian honeycreeper formerly common in Oahu's native forests. It disappeared by the 1980s, eliminated by introduced rats, avian malaria, and destruction of lowland forest.

children.name: Paroreomyza flammea
children.description: An extinct Hawaiian honeycreeper from Molokai, last recorded in 1963. The male was vivid orange-red (Hawaiian name: kakawahie). Eliminated by introduced rats and avian diseases.

children.name: Magumma
children.description: The Anianiau — the smallest Hawaiian honeycreeper and one of the few with a relatively stable population, confined to Kauai's native forest.

children.name: Magumma parva
children.description: The smallest Hawaiian honeycreeper, with males a brilliant lemon yellow. Stronghold in the Alaka'i Swamp on Kauai is above the mosquito line, giving it better protection than most Hawaiian honeycreepers.

children.name: Chloridops
children.description: The Kona Grosbeak — an extinct Hawaiian honeycreeper with an enormously massive bill for crushing the hard seeds of the naio tree, last seen in 1894.

children.name: Chloridops kona
children.description: An extinct Hawaiian honeycreeper with the most massive bill relative to body size of any Hawaiian finch. Specialised on the rock-hard seeds of the naio tree in the dry forests of the Kona coast. Last collected in 1894.

children.name: Rhodacanthis
children.description: Koa finches — extinct Hawaiian honeycreepers with large bills adapted for crushing native koa pods, eliminated by introduced rats and forest clearance in the 19th century.

children.name: Rhodacanthis flaviceps
children.description: An extinct Hawaiian honeycreeper from the koa forests of Hawaii island, last recorded in 1891. Orange-yellow head and olive-green body; fed primarily on koa caterpillars and seeds.

children.name: Rhodacanthis palmeri
children.description: The largest of the extinct koa finches, with a deep orange head and robust bill for crushing koa pods. Found on Hawaii island, last seen in 1896. Disappeared following introduced rats and widespread felling of koa forest for cattle pasture.

children.name: Loxioides
children.description: The Palila — a critically endangered Hawaiian honeycreeper entirely dependent on the mamane tree, and the subject of landmark environmental law cases in the USA.

children.name: Loxioides bailleui
children.description: A large, bright yellow-headed finch of mamane-naio woodland on the upper slopes of Mauna Kea, Hawaii. Entirely dependent on the mamane tree; it can detoxify the seedpods that are toxic to most birds. Total population under 2,000 individuals.

children.name: Chlorodrepanis
children.description: Hawaiian amakihis — adaptable honeycreepers showing documented genetic resistance to avian malaria, allowing recolonisation of lower elevations than other honeycreepers can survive.

children.name: Chlorodrepanis virens
children.description: The most widespread Hawaiian amakihi, found on Hawaii, Maui, Molokai, and Lanai. Feeds on nectar, insects, and spiders. Has shown documented genetic resistance to avian malaria — a rare hopeful sign for Hawaiian forest birds.

children.name: Chlorodrepanis flava
children.description: A small honeycreeper endemic to Oahu, one of the few native forest birds still found there. Populations in lower-elevation forests have developed partial malaria resistance and are recolonising areas below the original refugia.

children.name: Chlorodrepanis stejnegeri
children.description: The most distinctive amakihi, endemic to Kauai with a longer, more strongly curved bill. Common in the Alaka'i Wilderness; shows the same developing malaria resistance as the Oahu Amakihi.

children.name: Rhodospiza
children.description: The Desert Finch — a distinctive pale finch of Central Asian and Middle Eastern arid zones; the sole member of its genus.

children.name: Rhodospiza obsoleta
children.description: A pale sandy finch of arid steppe and semi-desert from Turkey and Iran east through Central Asia to Mongolia. The male has a pink blush on wing and tail feathers conspicuous in flight; breeds in isolated trees and shrubs in open desert.

children.name: Callacanthis
children.description: The Spectacled Finch — a striking high-altitude finch of Himalayan conifer forests, the male bearing bold red supercilia and red-spotted wings; the sole member of its genus.

children.name: Callacanthis burtoni
children.description: A distinctive finch of the western Himalayas from Pakistan and Kashmir to western Nepal, inhabiting subalpine conifer forests between 2,500 and 4,000 m. The male's scarlet supercilium and wing-spots make it one of the most handsome finches in Asia.

children.name: Pyrrhoplectes
children.description: The Gold-naped Finch — a jewel-like finch of dense Himalayan bamboo and rhododendron undergrowth, the male bearing a stunning gold-orange nape against jet-black plumage.

children.name: Pyrrhoplectes epauletta
children.description: A jewel-like finch of dense bamboo and rhododendron undergrowth in the eastern Himalayas from Nepal to southwest China, between 2,500 and 4,000 m. The male is jet-black with a vivid gold-orange nape patch; shy and often difficult to observe in dense vegetation.

children.name: Haematospiza
children.description: The Scarlet Finch — a vivid scarlet finch of Himalayan and Southeast Asian montane forests, the brilliantly coloured male one of the most eye-catching birds in the region.

children.name: Haematospiza sipahi
children.description: A stunning finch of montane broadleaf and mixed forest from the eastern Himalayas to Yunnan and Vietnam, the male entirely brilliant scarlet with a black mask and bill. Inhabits dense forest between 1,200 and 3,000 m; frequently encountered at flowering trees.

children.name: Uragus
children.description: The Long-tailed Rosefinch — a delicate, pale pink finch of willow and birch scrub along rivers across Siberia and East Asia, with a distinctive long, forked tail.

children.name: Uragus sibiricus
children.description: A graceful, pastel-pink finch of riverine willow and birch scrub across Siberia, Central Asia, and East Asia, the male washed in rosy-pink with a conspicuous long, notched tail. Commonly encountered in riverside scrub from western Siberia east to Japan and Korea.

children.name: Agraphospiza
children.description: The Crimson-browed Finch — a distinctive finch of dense Himalayan montane forest undergrowth, the male with a striking crimson forehead and orange-red breast.

children.name: Agraphospiza rubescens
children.description: A finch of dense rhododendron and bamboo in the eastern Himalayas and mountains of Yunnan and Sichuan, inhabiting forest between 2,500 and 4,000 m. The male has a vivid crimson forehead and orange-red breast.

children.name: Kozlowia
children.description: Roborowski's Rosefinch — a mysterious, little-known rosefinch of high-altitude Tibetan scrub, rarely observed even by experienced birders in the region.

children.name: Kozlowia roborowskii
children.description: One of the most poorly known rosefinches, found on the high-altitude plateau and alpine scrub of Qinghai and Tibet. Rarely observed; its ecology and breeding behaviour remain largely undocumented.

children.name: Linurgus
children.description: The Oriole Finch — a distinctively coloured African finch of highland forests, the male combining vivid yellow plumage with a black head, resembling a miniature oriole.

children.name: Linurgus olivaceus
children.description: A distinctive finch of montane forest edges from Nigeria and Cameroon east to Uganda and Tanzania. The male's vivid yellow plumage and jet-black head are unmistakable; inhabits forest canopy and edges from 1,000 to 3,000 m.

children.name: Neospiza
children.description: The Sao Tome Grosbeak — a critically endangered, large-billed finch endemic to São Tomé, one of the rarest birds in Africa.

children.name: Neospiza concolor
children.description: A critically endangered finch with a massive bill, endemic to São Tomé in the Gulf of Guinea. Known from only a few 19th-century specimens and a handful of modern sightings in primary montane forest. Its diet and breeding habits remain almost unknown.

children.name: Rhynchostruthus
children.description: Golden-winged grosbeaks and the Warsangli Linnet — finches of the Horn of Africa and Socotra island with heavy bills and, in one species, vivid yellow wing patches.

children.name: Rhynchostruthus socotranus
children.description: A striking finch endemic to the island of Socotra in the Arabian Sea, with bold yellow wing patches flashing conspicuously in flight. Found in dry woodland and scrub on the island plateau; one of many extraordinary Socotran endemics.

children.name: Rhynchostruthus louisae
children.description: One of the rarest and most poorly known finches in the world, restricted to the mountains of northeastern Somalia (Somaliland). Known from only a handful of specimens and sightings since 1903. Endangered by habitat loss and a tiny range.

children.name: Euphonia
children.description: Euphonias — vivid Neotropical finches now placed in Fringillidae by genomic data. Males are typically deep blue-black above with vivid yellow underparts. Specialists on mistletoe berries, they have evolved rapid gut passage to excrete seeds before toxins are absorbed, making them critical mistletoe dispersers in Neotropical forests.

children.name: Euphonia violacea
children.description: The most widespread euphonia, found from Trinidad and the Guianas south to Bolivia and Brazil. The male is deep purplish-blue above with vivid orange-yellow underparts. A characteristic bird of forest edges and gardens throughout northern South America.

children.name: Euphonia laniirostris
children.description: A euphonia of forest edges and gardens from Costa Rica to Bolivia and Brazil, recognised by its heavy bill and bright yellow forehead. Commonly encountered in secondary growth throughout its range.

children.name: Euphonia chlorotica
children.description: One of the most widespread euphonias in South America, found from Venezuela to Argentina in open woodland and cerrado. The male has a purple-blue gloss on the throat and a vivid yellow cap.

children.name: Euphonia xanthogaster
children.description: A euphonia of humid forest edges from Panama along the Andes to Bolivia, the male bearing bright orange-yellow underparts and a deep blue-black back. One of the most common euphonias in Andean foothill forest.

children.name: Euphonia gouldi
children.description: A euphonia of humid lowland forest in Central America from Mexico to Panama, distinctive in that both sexes have olive-green backs rather than the blue-black typical of the genus. A forest interior specialist.

children.name: Euphonia elegantissima
children.description: A euphonia of highland pine-oak and cloud forests in Mexico and Central America, the male with a vivid turquoise-blue cap contrasting with deep purple-blue upperparts. One of the most colourful euphonias.

children.name: Euphonia musica
children.description: The only euphonia native to the Caribbean, found on Hispaniola, Puerto Rico, and several Lesser Antilles. Formerly known as the Blue-hooded Euphonia from its striking turquoise crown.

children.name: Euphonia cyanocephala
children.description: A euphonia of subtropical and tropical forest from Colombia and Venezuela south to Argentina. The male has a distinctive blue cap and golden-yellow rump conspicuous in canopy feeding flocks.

children.name: Euphonia affinis
children.description: A euphonia of dry scrub and arid woodland in Mexico and Central America — one of the few that tolerates drier habitats. The male has a black throat contrasting with vivid yellow underparts.

children.name: Euphonia minuta
children.description: The smallest euphonia, a bird of humid lowland forest from Mexico south to Peru and Brazil. The male has white undertail coverts unique among euphonias.

children.name: Euphonia hirundinacea
children.description: One of the most common euphonias in Central America, found in forest edges, gardens, and secondary growth from Mexico to Panama. Frequently seen in urban parks where fruiting trees attract it.

children.name: Euphonia rufiventris
children.description: A euphonia of Amazonian forest with a distinctive orange-rufous belly distinguishing it from most relatives. Inhabits terra firme forest interior where it feeds on mistletoe and small berries.

children.name: Euphonia pectoralis
children.description: A euphonia of the Atlantic Forest of eastern Brazil and adjacent Argentina, the male bearing a rich chestnut belly unique among South American euphonias. A specialist of dense humid forest in the highly threatened Atlantic Forest biome.

children.name: Euphonia chalybea
children.description: A euphonia of the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, Uruguay, and northeastern Argentina. The male has a distinctive green-blue chin patch and is one of the largest euphonias. Near Threatened due to Atlantic Forest deforestation.

children.name: Euphonia concinna
children.description: A localised euphonia of Colombia's Magdalena Valley inhabiting dry forest and scrub. Uncommon due to severe fragmentation of its dry forest habitat.

children.name: Euphonia jamaica
children.description: A euphonia endemic to Jamaica, found in humid forest, woodland edges, and gardens across the island. The male has a yellow forehead and green-blue gloss lacking the deep blue-black of continental euphonias.

children.name: Euphonia fulvicrissa
children.description: The fulvous-vented euphonia is a species of bird in the family Fringillidae, the finches and euphonias. It is found in Colombia, Ecuador, and Panama.

children.name: Euphonia luteicapilla
children.description: A euphonia of humid forest in Costa Rica and Nicaragua, the male with a full yellow crown extending to the nape. Inhabits forest canopy and edges; less common than the Thick-billed Euphonia in most of its range.

children.name: Euphonia anneae
children.description: A euphonia of humid montane forest in Costa Rica and Panama, the male with an orange-tawny cap. Inhabits dense forest between 500 and 1,800 m; feeds on mistletoe and other small fruits in the forest canopy.

children.name: Euphonia imitans
children.description: A euphonia of humid lowland forest in Costa Rica and western Panama, the male distinguished by yellow spots on the forecrown. Uncommon to rare; inhabits forest interior and edges in the Pacific lowlands.

children.name: Euphonia saturata
children.description: A euphonia of the Pacific lowlands of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru, the male bearing a distinctive orange crown patch. Inhabits humid forest edges and secondary growth in the lowlands and foothill zone.

children.name: Euphonia cayennensis
children.description: A euphonia of humid forest in the Guianas, Venezuela, and northern Brazil, the male with golden-yellow sides and a deep blue-black back. Inhabits forest canopy and edges in the Amazonian lowlands.

children.name: Chlorophonia
children.description: Chlorophonias — vivid emerald-green Neotropical finches closely related to euphonias, now classified within Fringillidae. Males are brilliant green with yellow or blue highlights; females predominantly olive. Chlorophonias inhabit montane forests from Mexico to northern South America.

children.name: Chlorophonia cyanea
children.description: The most widespread chlorophonia, found in humid montane forest along the Andes from Venezuela to Bolivia and in the Atlantic Forest of southern Brazil. Males are vivid emerald-green with a blue nape patch and yellow breast-band.

children.name: Chlorophonia callophrys
children.description: A chlorophonia of highland cloud forests in Costa Rica and Panama, the male vivid green with a golden-yellow supercilium and blue crown. Inhabits dense mossy forest between 1,500 and 3,000 m.

children.name: Chlorophonia flavirostris
children.description: A chlorophonia of humid foothill and cloud forests from Colombia and Ecuador to Peru, the male bearing a vivid yellow collar on otherwise emerald-green plumage.

children.name: Chlorophonia occipitalis
children.description: A chlorophonia of highland cloud forests in Mexico and Guatemala, the male a brilliant emerald green with a vivid blue crown. Inhabits dense oak and cloud forest between 1,500 and 3,000 m.

children.name: Chlorophonia pyrrhophrys
children.description: A chlorophonia of Andean cloud forest in Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru, the male having a unique chestnut breast-band on vivid green and blue plumage. Inhabits epiphyte-laden forest at 1,500 to 2,500 m.
